author: @feiabm2005
text: Thank You Sir

author: @MultiOutdoorman
text: If you hold the can vertically (nozzle at the bottom) you wont expel too much propellant and the contents will go further...

author: @BillHartCooks.
text:
Cool....  I want to come play in your workshop.  I have been trying to come up with a version of this for a while.  The Archimedes Screw Generator, not the foam forming.  Where I live we get a pretty good amount of rain, and sometimes several inches within hours.  
Anyway watching all that water run made me think of capturing some of the energy and I figured a series of switch backs over a relatively  steep but shallow slope would allow the same water to turn multiple generators.  
I tried to do some of the math but I am not sure if I got it right, but I am thinking that the longer the screw (with more coils) the more force could be captured from the same amount of water...
Anyway This series is very cool to me... Keep at it

replies.author: @TimeSurfer206
replies.text:
Do you live in Skedaddle, Warshington, too?
Because every time it rains here, I'm looking at the off-flow from the roof of the (unfinished) house I'm next to, and think about all that wasted energy.
This looks like the way to go.

author: @marcelinuskevin631
text: Hi, awesome video. Just to make sure, what is the material that you use for the frame or the body?

author: @TimothySkinner_photographer
text: Have you considered putting magnets on the edge of the screw and embedding the coils in the base and walls rather than attaching to the axle to get the highest RPM for the generator? It may provide increased electrical output?

author: @endadalton
text: Totally ingenious

author: @conductiveinkalternative918
text: Even airflow will turn this from all directions!

author: @BoB4jjjjs
text: Some smooth flue pipe would be close to what you need, but any sheet metal works would roll you a pipe in no time, though it would have a joint, it could be on outside and smoothed on inside.  I wish I was near a stream I could play with that and a water wheel to produce my own power, and a wind generator.

author: @Flashahol
text: All I see is just brilliant hacks to make anything I may think of.

author: @DeliciousDeBlair
text: I've used that type form [as well as aerosol foams professionally in the construction industry, and they make one which expands less, but also sags less, so you can use it specifically for making shapes from, without it falling off before taking the shape you wanted and hardening.

author: @William_Hada
text: I really like that technique of using expanding foam to form rigid bulky structures!

author: @thornhedge9639
text: Yup, I looked at prices on those screws just for Kicks (forget I mentioned it)  Great build by the way!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @thornhedge9639
text: Hey Robert; the Screw you made is a piece of standard equipment in grain silo loading setups.  Is is available through Ag supply companies and is metal to boot.  Bonus: it is available in  the correctly sized pipe. Just in case you are interested.

author: @hissst69
text: Awesomely simplified !  I wonder if you are going to do some more electronics too? I really enjoyed the energy harvester and doing bits and bobs with motors, making small circuits and such, would love to see how you would approach finding resonance in a tank circuit for example if one didn't have all the special equipment to find such resonance, and even a demonstration of a circuit out of resonance vs all tuned up. Regardless, I love what you do and happy to watch you in any direction you go as long as you continue having fun and making the world a simpler place :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: there is a lot to do mate but I will get round to it sooner or later I would think

author: @curtisnixon5313
text: And that, Ladies and Gentlemen, is how they built the pyramids.

author: @Killianwsh
text: Great build Rob!

author: @punchogunter2362
text: How can I build a generator powerful enough to power my home?????

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: build a big one of these if you have water - a big wind generator if you don't or buy a lot of solar panel - if you have never built anything before you might want to start small just to get your hand in so to speak

author: @mikeyearwood
text: Very clever.

author: @Cooliemasteroz
text: Hey Rob l’ve had a thought that won’t go away. What if you made one that had a spiral that went from a large diameter at one end down to a small diameter on at the other end inside a cone. I was thinking that if it were used to generate power from water then with the large diameter at the top it might not need to be as long as a parallel set up and be able to work as well if not better with a greatly variable water flow. I suppose an experienced mechanical engineer would know the answer but it’s good to experiment.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's interesting mate and very do able with his technique I would think - you would make the central core a cone and follow it round - itch would probably change thought

author: @Cooliemasteroz
text: You’re a true craftsman, well done.

author: @docink6175
text: nicely done, most expanding foams will kick off better if there is moisture, mist each layer and let it expand before adding another

author: @chuxxsss
text: Do you ever sleep?

author: @iamkian
text: Can you make a water wave generator?

author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text: Great series.  I've always wondered if a multiple helix screw would work in this sort of application. Archimedes seems to have missed that with the correct pitch, one can fit more than one screw over the same shaft.  I know that it works for some types of hardware and auger feeds.  Looking forward to more.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you could do multiple flights for sure mate

author: @garywillis5790
text: Cracking idea! Looking forward to seeing it working!

author: @Barskor1
text: Nice I think they call that foam Great Stuff and using it for sealing window frames in houses in the USA.

replies.text: they use it for the same thing here mate - but it's just called expanding filler or expanding foam

author: @BushImports
text: Nicely done.

author: @Buzzhumma
text: I have always felt they would be great in downpipes from the roof rain as they already have an enclosure but under heavy rain they would not dissipate the flow fast enough so i thought an second external downpipe would be the answer !

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I don't know mate - worth trying it I would think

author: @kenrowe167
text: Hi Rob. Looking at commercial archimedes hydrotubines, they all seem to have 3 flights intertwined co-axially, so that there are effectively 3 inlets for the water at the head of the penstock. I couldn't find any paper on this, so I don't know if this arrangement increases the efficiency, the power output or both. But they must be doing it for a good reason.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I did see that mate - well worth adding another one for sure

author: @tombrooks2015
text: always have the can upside down or you will not use all the foam ;)

replies.text: And moist the surface u spray first with water it will expand better and more with less.

author: @stevetobias4890
text: Very cool. Something like this could be adapted to the downpipes to use the rainfall from your gutters to generate power. Even using a natural fall in a creek on your property would generate power as long as water is flowing. Very cool indeed, I can think of so many uses for this.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I would like to see that - I am sort of hoping someone will take up that challenge mate - hint hint lol

author: @Buzzhumma
text: The best thing about the screw is the water  continues to exert pressure on the entirety of surface creating turning force for the whole length unlike a propeller design which once done with the small surface is forever lost  and as it is enclosed for the area with water, there is minimal angular loss as opposed to a propeller that whisks the water away to the outside reducing effeciency!

author: @joohop
text:
Smart Work Rob . I've Thought Putting That Expanding Foam In My 2 Man Inflatable Kayak For Rigidity,  Mmmm We'll See ....
Bless Up

replies.author: @brainretardant
replies.text: Make sure you use closed cell foam.  Some foam actually gas a sponge network that will fill with water.  I'm going pool noodle on an old zodiac

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: this type needs exposure to the air in order to cure mate - so I would think you would need a two part type that didn't need that - but you can buy them for sure
